EX: Am I tall?
→ Yes, you're tall.
→ No, you're not tall.

EX: Are you a student?
→ Yes, I am.
→ No, I am not.

rows.subject: Is | He/She/It

EX: Is it beautiful?

→ No, it isn't.

EX: He goes to school by bike.
Exception
EX: She has a new dress. (have → has)

rows.subject: I / You / We / They

rows.subject: He / She / It
rows.example: EX: She doesn't buy that flower.

rows.subject: Do | I / You / We / They

EX: Do you play soccer?
→ Yes, we do.

rows.subject: Does | He / She / It
rows.example:
EX: Does he walk to school everyday?
→ No, he doesn't.

blocks.intro:
(Instructed by Otto Trung)
The present simple is used to express an action that is regular, habitual or repeated, or to express truth and obvious facts.

items.label: SIGNAL WORDS
bullets: When the sentence contains adverbs of frequency: Always, usually, often, sometimes, frequently, seldom, rarely, hardly, never, generally, regularly...
Every day, week, month, year...
Once, twice, three times, four times.... a day, week, month, year...

items.label: POSITION OF FREQUENCY ADVERBS IN PRESENT SIMPLE
bullets: These adverbs (Always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, seldom...) usually appear before main verbs and after auxiliary verbs (be, do, have).

a) Add s to the end of most verbs:
EX: want → wants; work → works; ...

b) Add es to verbs ending in ch, sh, s, ss, x, z, o:
EX: miss → misses; wash → washes; fix → fixes; teach → teaches; go → goes ...

c) Remove y and add ies after verbs ending in a consonant + y:
EX: study → studies; fly → flies; try → tries ...

table:
When the word ends with /f/, /t/, /k/, /p/, /θ/
EX: stops, works, months...

When the word ends with /s/, /z/, /ʃ/, /tʃ/, /ʒ/, /dʒ/
→ (often ends with letters ce, x, z, sh, ch, s, ge)
EX: changes, wishes...

When the word ends with a vowel and the remaining consonants
EX: dreams, belongs...
blocks.note: Note: It is important to check the dictionary to determine the final sounds and apply the rules above accurately.

rows.example: EX: I'm studying Math now.

Singular nouns
Uncountable nouns

EX: Is John playing football now?
→ No, he isn't.

rows.subject:
Are | You/We/They
Plural nouns
rows.example: EX: Are they singing now?

rows.subject: Wh- + am/are/is (not) + S
rows.example:
EX: What are you doing now?
EX: Who is she studying with?

blocks.intro:
(Instructed by Otto Trung)
The present continuous is used to express actions that are happening at the moment of speaking or around the time of speaking, and the action is not yet finished (still continuing).

items.label: Signal words
bullets:
a) When the sentence contains adverbs of time:
- Now, right now, at this moment, at present
- Today, this week...
- It's + time + now (EX: It's 12 o'clock now)
- Look!, Watch!, Listen!...

b) In the sentence appears verbs such as:
- Keep silent! EX: The teacher is saying the main point of the lesson!
- Watch out! (= Look out!) EX: Watch out! The train is coming!

items.label: Note
bullets: Do not use the present continuous with verbs that express perception, awareness (Stative Verbs) such as: be, see, hear, feel, realize, seem, remember, forget, understand, know, like, want, glance, smell, love, hate, ...

blocks.description:
Stative verbs are verbs that express a state.
Stative verbs are often related to: emotions, existence, desire, sense, possession, thoughts/opinion or measurement.
blocks.note: Stative verbs are often not used in the continuous tense.

vocabulary.meaning: something that you do often and regularly, sometimes without knowing that you are doing it
vocabulary.example: Eating healthy is a good habit.

vocabulary.meaning: lasting for a long time or forever; not temporary
vocabulary.example: This is my permanent address.

vocabulary.meaning: not often; seldom
vocabulary.example: She rarely goes out at night.

vocabulary.meaning: to make someone feel slightly angry or irritated
vocabulary.example: Stop annoying me!

vocabulary.meaning: a sign, word, or text that tells you what to do next
vocabulary.example: Follow the prompt to complete the action.

vocabulary.meaning: hot food that is served very quickly in a restaurant and can be taken away
vocabulary.example: Fast-food is not good for health.

vocabulary.meaning: to wash your body while standing under a spray of water
vocabulary.example: I always have a shower in the morning.

vocabulary.meaning: the sister of your father or mother, or the wife of your uncle
vocabulary.example: My aunt lives in Hanoi.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who writes, sings, or plays music, especially as a job
vocabulary.example: He is a talented musician.

vocabulary.meaning: to say something that you know is not true; a statement that is not true
vocabulary.example: Don't tell a lie.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who tells lies and does not speak the truth
vocabulary.example: No one believes a liar.

vocabulary.meaning: to put your hand or fingers on something
vocabulary.example: Don't touch that hot pan!

vocabulary.meaning: a small, round object that you press to operate a machine or piece of equipment
vocabulary.example: Press the button to start.

vocabulary.meaning: a household machine used for washing clothes
vocabulary.example: Put the clothes in the washing machine.

vocabulary.meaning: a building or room containing collections of books and periodicals for people to read or borrow
vocabulary.example: I study in the library.

vocabulary.meaning: an organized journey for a particular purpose, especially to explore a new place
vocabulary.example: They went on an expedition to the jungle.

vocabulary.meaning: a tropical forest where plants and trees grow very thickly together
vocabulary.example: The jungle is full of wild animals.

vocabulary.meaning: to crush something, or to force yourself into a very small space
vocabulary.example: People squashed into the train.

vocabulary.meaning: a game played by two people in a closed court using rackets and a small rubber ball
vocabulary.example: He plays squash every weekend.

vocabulary.meaning: worried, unhappy, or angry about something bad that has happened
vocabulary.example: She was upset about the news.

vocabulary.meaning: to form a word with the correct letters in the correct order
vocabulary.example: How do you spell your name?

vocabulary.meaning: a warm piece of clothing with long sleeves, made of wool or cotton
vocabulary.example: Put on a sweater, it's cold.

vocabulary.meaning: a toy made of light frame covered with paper or cloth, flown in the air on a long string
vocabulary.example: They are flying a kite.

vocabulary.meaning: the scientific study of the stars, planets, and other natural objects in space
vocabulary.example: He loves studying astronomy.

vocabulary.meaning: a board game for two people played with dice and counters
vocabulary.example: Do you know how to play backgammon?

vocabulary.meaning: to send something through the air with force, especially by using your arm and hand
vocabulary.example: Throw the ball to me.

vocabulary.meaning: a small cube with dots on each side, used in games of chance
vocabulary.example: Roll the dice to see who goes first.

vocabulary.meaning: a game for two players who move 16 pieces each according to specific rules on a checkered board
vocabulary.example: They play chess every afternoon.

blocks.content: 2. HOW TO CHANGE VERBS INTO THE PAST

bullets:
a) Add ed to the end of the verb:
EX: watch → watched; turn → turned; want → wanted; attach → attached

b) If the verb ends with "e", just add "d":
EX: type → typed; smile → smiled; agree → agreed

c) If the verb has ONE syllable, ends with ONE consonant, and has ONE vowel before the consonant, double the last consonant and add "-ed":
EX: stop → stopped; shop → shopped; tap → tapped
* Note: Some words don't follow this rule: commit → committed; travel → travelled; prefer → preferred

d) Verbs ending with "y":
- If "y" is preceded by a vowel (e, u, o, a, i), keep "y" and add ed. EX: enjoy → enjoyed; play → played; stay → stayed
- If "y" is preceded by a consonant, change "y" to "i" and add ed. EX: carry → carried; study → studied; hurry → hurried

table:
When the verb ends with the sound /t/, /d/
EX: noted, added, decided...

When the verb ends with the sound /ch/, /s/, /x/, /sh/, /k/, /f/, /p/
EX: watched, fixed, coughed...

When the verb ends with the remaining consonants and vowels
EX: smiled, played...

bullets:
To learn and look up irregular verbs (V2/V3) quickly, you can open the online lookup table here:
- [List of irregular verbs](https://cdn.thuvienphapluat.vn/phap-luat/2022-2/NHPT/bang-360-dong-tu-bat-quy-tac-tvpl.pdf)
- [Oxford Dictionary](https://www.oxfordlearnersdictionaries.com/)

items.label: Main Usage
bullets:
a) Expressing an action that happened and ended in the past:
EX: He visited his school yesterday.
EX: She came to her grandmother's house last week.

b) Expressing a series of actions that happened in the past:
EX: She went home, switched on her laptop and checked her emails first.

c) Expressing an action that interrupted an ongoing action in the past:
EX: When I was cooking, my parents came.

items.label: Signal words
bullets:
a) When the sentence contains adverbs of past time:
- yesterday, ago, last (week, year, month), in the past, the day before
- time periods that have passed in the day (today, this morning, this afternoon)
- an hour/ a week/ a year ago...

b) After words/structures such as:
- as if, as though (as if)
- it's time (it's time to)
- if only, wish (wish)
- would sooner/ rather (prefer)

rows.subject:
He/She/It
Singular Nouns
Uncountable Nouns
rows.example: EX: The cat was sleeping when we came home.

rows.subject:
You/We/They
Plural Nouns
rows.example: EX: We were just talking about it before you arrived.

rows.subject:
He/She/It
Singular Nouns
Uncountable Nouns
rows.example: EX: He wasn't working when his boss came yesterday.

rows.subject:
You/We/They
Plural Nouns
rows.example: EX: They weren't practicing for their match.

rows.subject:
Was | He/She/It
Singular Nouns
Uncountable Nouns
rows.example:
EX: Was your grandmother going to Ben Thanh market at 7 A.M. yesterday?
→ Yes, she was.
→ No, she wasn't.

rows.subject:
Were | You/We/They
Plural Nouns
rows.example: EX: The sun was rising, and the birds were singing this morning!

rows.subject: Wh- + was/were + S
rows.example: EX: Why were you continuously arguing this issue?

bullets:
a) Expressing an action that was happening at a specific time in the past:
EX: At 9 A.M. yesterday, he was watching Spider Man.

b) Expressing two actions happening at the same time in the past:
EX: While I was taking a bath, she was using my computer.

c) Expressing an action that was happening when another action interrupted it:
EX: When I was cooking, the light went out.

d) Expressing repeated actions in the past that annoyed someone:
EX: When he worked here, he was always making noise.

items.label: Signal words
bullets:
a) Time adverbs in the past with a specific time:
- at + hour + past time (at 12 o'clock last night, ...)
- at this time + past time (at this time two weeks ago, ...)
- an hour/ a week/ a year ago... in + year (in 2000, in 2005)
b) In sentences with "when" or "while" indicating an ongoing action interrupted by another action.

items.label: Special Notes
bullets:
When encountering: While; When; at that time; ... → consider using past continuous.
- EX: The man was sending his letter in the post office at that time.
- EX: She was dancing while I was singing.

rows.subject: S + used to + V (bare)
rows.example:
EX: We used to go to school together.
(We used to go to school together.)

rows.subject: S + didn't + use to + V (bare)
rows.example:
EX: We didn't use to be vegetarians.
(We didn't use to be vegetarians.)

rows.subject: Did + S + use to + V (bare)?
rows.example:
EX: Did he use to work in the office very late at night?
(Did he use to work in the office very late at night?)

bullets:
a) To express a habit in the past:
EX: I used to walk to school when I was a child.

b) To express a state / condition in the past:
EX: She used to have long hair but nowadays this hair is very short.

blocks.content: 3. COMPARE USED TO - BE USED TO - GET USED TO

rows:
be used to + N / V-ing
not used to + N / V-ing
Used to refer to a past habit that is no longer practiced.
Expresses familiarity with something that has been done repeatedly, indicating no longer strange or difficult.
Expresses a process of gradually becoming accustomed to something.

EX: When I was 6, I used to eat ice-cream everyday.
(When I was 6, I used to eat ice-cream everyday.)

EX: I am used to getting up lately in the morning. (I'm used to getting up lately in the morning...)

EX: I am not used to the new system in the factory yet. (I'm not used to the new system...)

EX: He gets used to American food.
(He gets used to American food.)

blackboard.title: Examples (Illustrations):
items: Used to: "When I was teenager, I used to eat pizza almost every day."
Get used to: "I get used to using chopsticks!"
Be used to ...: "I'm used to getting up lately in the morning."
Not be used to ...: "I'm not used to the new system in the factory yet."

vocabulary.meaning: on a lower floor of a building

vocabulary.meaning: worried or anxious

vocabulary.meaning: yellow fat food made from milk

vocabulary.meaning: unplanned event causing damage or injury

vocabulary.meaning: feeling wonder at something unexpected

vocabulary.meaning: sad because hopes were not met

vocabulary.meaning: pale yellow or gold hair

vocabulary.meaning: leader of a sports team or ship

vocabulary.meaning: something difficult that tests your ability

vocabulary.meaning: winner of a competition

vocabulary.meaning: be dishonest to win or get an advantage

vocabulary.meaning: traditional orchestral music

vocabulary.meaning: organization for people with shared interests

vocabulary.meaning: event where people try to win

vocabulary.meaning: interesting and enjoyable

EX: I only play games for fun.

EX: We arrived just in time for the film.

wordFormation.meaning: act: perform or take action / active: busy doing things / inactive: not doing anything / actor or actress: person who performs in a play or movie
wordFormation.example:
EX: He acted very well in the play.
EX: She's an active member of the club.
EX: My favorite actor is Johnny Depp.

wordFormation.meaning: athlete: person who plays sports / athletic: strong and good at sports / athletics: sports like running and jumping
wordFormation.example:
EX: The athlete won a gold medal.
EX: She has an athletic build.
EX: Athletics is popular at school.

wordFormation.meaning: children: more than one child / childhood: the time when you are a child
wordFormation.example:
EX: The children are playing in the park.
EX: I had a very happy childhood.

wordFormation.meaning: collection: group of similar objects / collector: person who collects things
wordFormation.example:
EX: She has a collection of old coins.
EX: He is a stamp collector.

wordFormation.meaning: entertain: invite people and give them food or fun / entertainment: shows, films, or games that interest people
wordFormation.example:
EX: He entertained us with his stories.
EX: There is live entertainment tonight.

wordFormation.meaning: heroic: very brave / hero: brave man or main male character / heroine: brave woman or main female character
wordFormation.example:
EX: He made a heroic rescue attempt.
EX: The hero of the movie is a young detective.
EX: She is a national heroine.

wordFormation.meaning: musical: relating to music / musician: person who plays or writes music
wordFormation.example:
EX: He plays a musical instrument.
EX: She is a talented musician.

wordFormation.meaning: player: person who plays a game / playful: full of fun and wanting to play
wordFormation.example:
EX: He is a tennis player.
EX: The puppy is very playful.

wordFormation.meaning: sailing: sport of using boats with sails / sailor: person who works on a ship
wordFormation.example:
EX: They went sailing in the lake.
EX: The sailor steered the ship.

wordFormation.meaning: sing: make musical sounds with the voice / singer: person who sings / singing: activity of making musical sounds with the voice
wordFormation.example:
EX: She loves to sing pop songs.
EX: He is a famous singer.
EX: I heard singing next door.

wordPatterns.example: EX: The boys are bored with staying at home all day.

wordPatterns.meaning: very excited or enthusiastic about something
wordPatterns.example: EX: Tom is crazy about football.
EX: Helen is good at Maths (doing Maths).

wordPatterns.meaning: wanting to learn or know more about something

wordPatterns.example: EX: I am keen on reading books.

wordPatterns.meaning: liked by a specific group of people
wordPatterns.example: EX: This game is popular with teenagers.

wordPatterns.meaning: want to have or do something
wordPatterns.example: EX: I feel like a complete idiot.

wordPatterns.meaning: pay attention to sounds or music
wordPatterns.example: EX: I'm fond of listening to folk music.

wordPatterns.meaning: join or participate in an event
wordPatterns.example: EX: She wants to take part in the competition.

wordPatterns.meaning: written work describing a specific topic
wordPatterns.example: EX: He wrote a book about chess.

wordPatterns.meaning: person who admires someone or something greatly
wordPatterns.example: EX: I am a fan of classical music.

wordPatterns.meaning: a match played in competition with another side
wordPatterns.example: EX: We played a game against the champions.
